title: MiCADangelo: Fine-Grained Reconstruction of Constrained CAD Models from 3D Scans
abstract: Computer-Aided Design (CAD) plays a foundational role in modern manufacturing and product development, often requiring designers to modify or build upon existing models. Converting 3D scans into parametric CAD representations—a process known as CAD reverse engineering—remains a significant challenge due to the high precision and structural complexity of CAD models. Existing deep learning-based approaches typically fall into two categories: bottom-up, geometry-driven methods, which often fail to produce fully parametric outputs, and top-down strategies, which tend to overlook fine-grained geometric details. Moreover, current methods neglect an essential aspect of CAD modeling: sketch-level constraints. In this work, we introduce a novel approach to CAD reverse engineering inspired by how human designers manually perform the task. Our method leverages multi-plane cross-sections to extract 2D patterns and capture fine parametric details more effectively. It enables the reconstruction of detailed and editable CAD models, outperforming state-of-the-art methods and, for the first time, incorporating sketch constraints directly into the reconstruction process.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This work approaches parametric CAD reverse engineering, with a particular focus on sketch-extrusion models. The paper introduces a cross-section-based approach for sketch prediction and constraints discovery. Quantitative evaluations demonstrate superior performance compared to prior works, while qualitative results indicate an enhanced capability in handling more complex CAD models than existing approaches.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths
+ This work incorporates sketch constraint modeling into 3D CAD reverse engineering, which looks interesting but lacks further constraint optimization.
+ During extrusion optimization, the method jointly optimizes multiple extrusion heights to enhance stability and performance. Experiments (e.g., Fig. 6) demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach.

Weakness
+ The idea of using cross-section has been explored in several prior works, such as CAD-SIGNet[1], PS-CAD[2], and PlankAssembly[3]. This paper lacks a detailed discussion on how its approach differs from or improves upon these works, raising concerns about the contribution. 
Furthermore, due to the hierarchical and nested nature of CAD construction steps, cross sections cannot fully capture all sketch information. For instance, the sketch of a cut feature may not be visible in the CAD geometry or any extracted cross-section.
+ The modeling of sketch constraints is incremental with a naive network. Similar constraint modeling techniques have been extensively explored in prior works [4, 5] on 2D sketches, and it is uncertain about its novelty. It would be interesting to incorporate sketch constraints for better sketch prediction, but I did not see explicit designs to explore this possiblity. 
+ The paper does not justify the advantages of its differentiable extrusion optimization over prior approaches. Prior works, e.g. Point2Cyl and SECAD-Net, employ an implicit function-based optimization strategy. From a practical standpoint, implicit methods are considered more robust since they can better handle noisy input. Why does this work adopt an explicit optimization formulation?

[1] Khan, Mohammad Sadil, et al. "Cad-signet: Cad language inference from point clouds using layer-wise sketch instance guided attention." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2024.

[2] Yang, Bingchen, et al. "PS-CAD: Local Geometry Guidance via Prompting and Selection for CAD Reconstruction." ACM Transactions on Graphics 44.3 (2025): 1-21.

[3] Hu, Wentao, et al. "Plankassembly: Robust 3d reconstruction from three orthographic views with learnt shape programs." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision. 2023.

[4] Yang, Yuezhi, and Hao Pan. "Discovering design concepts for cad sketches." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 35 (2022): 28803-28814.

[5] Seff, Ari, et al. "Vitruvion: A generative model of parametric cad sketches." arXiv preprint arXiv:2109.14124 (2021).

QUESTIONS:
+ Since the plane detection network is trained on DeepCAD dataset, the reviewer is curious about its generality on Fusion360 and CC3D dataset, an evaluation is suggested. 

+ The paper would benefit from a visual result illustrating how a complete reconstruction is decomposed into a set of sketch-extrusions. Such a figure should clearly highlight the intermediate sketches and their corresponding extrusions, including at least one example of a cut extrusion.

LIMITATIONS:
The authors have discussed the limitations of orthogonal plane detections. It would be interesting to see discussions about the interference of different extrusions during the optimization process.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper proposes a new CAD reverse engineering method, MiCADangelo, that reconstructs fully parametric CAD models from 3D scans. This method addresses the limitations of existing techniques by preserving fine geometric details and incorporating sketch constraints. Specifically, it analyzes 3D scans via 2D cross-sections to identify sketch planes, extracts closed loops into parametric curves, and optimizes extrusions based on local mesh geometry. Both quantitative and qualitative experiments demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing methods in CAD reverse modeling.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
1. This paper introduces a new and effective method that incorporates sketch-level constraints for CAD reverse engineering to improve the performance.
2. Overall, this paper is well organized, and the proposed method is easy to understand.

Weaknesses:
1. While the method is generally well-explained, some details of the constrained sketch parameterization network and the loss functions are not clear to me. For example, in the second term of the loss function, why do we need to minimize the square of Hi, and what does the subscript i refer to?
2. For the quantitative evaluation, I think the mean Chamfer distance results should be provided, but I only found the median Chamfer distance results.
3. For the evaluation of "Robustness to Real-world Scans", are there any testing results for inputs with noisy or incomplete 3D scans? I think it is common in real scenarios.
4. Since the sketch planes are detected from a set of 2D cross-section slices that are equally sampled along three axes, I wonder how to ensure the desired sketch planes fall in these?
5. As the limitation stated in this paper, this method only supports extrusion, and extrusions are defined based on sketch plane normals, which can be suboptimal for models with non-axis-aligned extrusions.

QUESTIONS:
As listed in the weaknesses section, I would like to know the answers to the first 4 points, i.e.,
1. While the method is generally well-explained, some details of the constrained sketch parameterization network and the loss functions are not clear to me. For example, in the second term of the loss function, why do we need to minimize the square of Hi, and what does the subscript i refer to?
2. For the quantitative evaluation, I think the mean Chamfer distance results should be provided, but I only found the median Chamfer distance results.
3. For the evaluation of "Robustness to Real-world Scans", are there any testing results for inputs with noisy or incomplete 3D scans? I think it is common in real scenarios.
4. Since the sketch planes are detected from a set of 2D cross-section slices that are equally sampled along three axes, I wonder how to ensure the desired sketch planes fall in these?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper proposes MiCADangelo, a novel framework for CAD reverse engineering that mimics human design workflows. Given a 3D mesh from a scan, the method selects key cross-sections, infers constrained 2D sketches, and optimizes extrusion parameters to construct a fully parametric and editable CAD model. The method also incorporates CAD sketch constraints into 3D CAD reconstruction. Experiments on DeepCAD and Fusion360 demonstrate significant improvements over prior work in both reconstruction quality and downstream editability.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
- The framework effectively bridges the gap between 3D scanning and CAD modeling, making it easier for designers to work with scanned data.
- The incorporation of CAD sketch constraints allows for more intuitive and flexible design workflows.
- Experimental results show significant improvements in reconstruction quality and editability compared to prior methods.

Weaknesses:
- The reliance on key cross-sections may limit the framework's ability to capture complex geometries. The performance of the framework on highly detailed or intricate models is not fully explored. For example, the method only handles extrusion-based models, which may not be suitable for all types of CAD designs (such as lofts or sweeps).
- Most parts of the method are rule-based, and the entire pipeline consists of multiple steps that might introduce cumulative errors. It would be interesting to have some high-level schematic comparisons w.r.t. other rule-based methods in terms of pipeline design choices.

QUESTIONS:
- How does the method handle imperfect scans (with holes and mis-oriented normals), or scans with noise? It seems that the proposed stages are mostly sensitive to noise and it would be nice to show how robust the method is towards these issues.
- How does the method perform on more complex geometries with multiple parts and intricate details? It would be interesting to apply the method to a larger mechanics and test the reconstruction quality and editability.

LIMITATIONS:
yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This work addresses the task of CAD reconstruction from 3D scans or meshes and proposes a novel approach that incorporates sketch constraints into the reconstruction process. Extensive experiments are conducted to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
*Strengths*:

1. The paper is clearly structured and well-written. Furthermore, the notation and symbols are clearly defined and explained, making the methodology easy to follow.

2. To my knowledge, the proposed method is novel. The pipeline emulates the workflow of human designers, which is both intuitive and reasonable.

3. The model demonstrates impressive performance. As shown in Figures 3, 4, and 7, the proposed method significantly outperforms baseline methods, particularly on complex models with fine-grained geometric details.

*Weaknesses*:

1. **Effect of Sketch Constraints**: While the authors state that incorporating sketch constraints benefits CAD reconstruction, the mechanism by which this improvement occurs is not sufficiently clear. Although quantitative results are presented in Table 3, additional visualizations or analysis of intermediate steps would help to better demonstrate the practical impact of sketch constraints on the CAD construction process.

2. **Pipeline Complexity and Error Accumulation**: The proposed method comprises four distinct steps, making it notably more complex than end-to-end baselines such as [15]. Since these steps are performed sequentially, there is a risk of error accumulation throughout the pipeline, which is not thoroughly analyzed or discussed.

3. **Role and Impact of Contextual Embeddings**: As indicated in Table 4, contextual embeddings are important for model performance. However, the authors only report the quantitative results of this step. It would be helpful to provide qualitative examples or further analysis of how contextual embeddings affect the quality of reconstructed CAD models. Additionally, the reported precision and recall are below 90%, which is relatively low. Does this limitation explain the model's weaker performance on the IR metric?

QUESTIONS:
1. How does the choice of N (as mentioned in Line 146) affect the model's performance?

2. Were all models (e.g., DeepCAD, CAD-Diffuser) retrained on the same training data for fair comparison?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
The submission received four positive recommendations. The reviewers liked the idea, the writing, and the performance improvement. At the same time, the reviewers were also concerned about the novelty over the previous methods, the justification, and the performance on real-world data. I believe the authors did a good job of addressing reviewers’ concerns in the rebuttal and author-reviewer discussion period. The reviewers reached a consensus without a brief discussion among reviewers afterwards. The AC also read through the manuscript, all reviews, the rebuttal, discussions, the confidential comments to AC, and the final justifications. The AC agreed with the reviewers and made a decision of acceptance. The decision was approved by the Senior AC.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
